coredata.dc:title: Glutaraldehyde non-conjugated chitosan polymer fluorophores for selective determination of picric acid via fluorescence resonance energy transfer strategy
coredata.dc:creator: He, Hongkun
Qi, Wenjing
Fu, Yuling
Zhao, Maoyu
Qi, Lin
Li, Rong
coredata.dc:description: Water-dispersed glutaraldehyde (GA) non-conjugated chitosan polymer fluorophores (GCPF) with quantum yield of 16% is synthesized by stirring chitosan and GA for 6 h at room temperature in the present work. It is a facile and mild method and fluorescent GCPF can be stabled for two months. Owing to the spectral overlap of fluorescent spectrum of GCPF and absorption spectrum of picric acid (PA), a novel sensitive fluorescent method using fluorescent GCPF for PA detection from 10 nM to 50 μM via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) strategy is established. The distance between donor of GCPF and acceptor of PA (R0 value) is calculated to be 3.5 nm. FRET method using fluorescent GCPF possesses high sensitivity (LOD of 2.8 nM), and selectivity and fast response within 2 min. Moreover, fluorescent GCPF is also utilized in visual analysis of PA using cotton swabs. Fluorescence quenching effect can be observed by eyes irradiated with 365 nm ultraviolet light at cotton swabs and using GCPF solid on quartz glasses, which paves an effect and wide way for the application of fluorescent GCPF in our daily life.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glutaraldehyde non-conjugated chitosan polymer fluorophores (GCPF)
Chitosan
Glutaraldehyde (GA)
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)
Picric acid (PA)

Synthesis and characterizations of fluorescent GCPF
Optimization of FRET detection of PA using fluorescent GCPF
Selectivity and sensitivity of FRET detection of PA using fluorescent GCPF
Application of FRET PA detection in real samples
FRET detection of PA using cotton swabs and GCPF solid on quartz glasses
Mechanism of PA detection using fluorescent GCPF

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images, fluorescent excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of the synthesized fluorescent GCPF. The right pictures represent the photographs of fluorescent GCPF under the irradiation of visible light (A) and 365 nm ultraviolet light (B); λex (nm): 340, 350, 360, 370, 380, 390 and 400; λem: 455 nm.
The absorption spectra of GCPF solution and PA. c(PA, μM): 50.

Kinetic behavior of FRET detection for PA using fluorescent GCPF. Black curve represents fluorescent intensity of the control (fluorescent GCPF); Red curve represents fluorescent intensity after the addition of PA; Time (min): immediately, 1, 2, 4, 6, 10, 15, 20, 25 and 30; c(PA, μM): 50; 0.04 M Phosphate buffer: pH 6.6; λ ex: 370 nm; λ em: 455 nm; Temperature (oC): 25; All the error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescent spectra (A) and linear calibration curve (B) of PA detection. I 0 represents fluorescent intensity of GCPF; I represents fluorescent intensity of GCPF and PA mixtures; (I 0-I)/I 0 represents fluorescence quenching efficiency of PA to GCPF; c(PA, μM): 0, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 30 and 50; 0.04 M phosphate buffer: pH 6.6; λ ex: 370 nm; λ em: 455 nm; Temperature (oC): 25; Reaction time (min): 2; All the error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.

Selectivity investigations of FRET detection for PA using fluorescent GCPF. I 0 represents fluorescent intensity of GCPF; I represents fluorescent intensity of GCPF and PA mixtures; (I 0-I)/I 0 represents fluorescence quenching efficiency of PA to GCPF; All the concentrations of PA, metal ions and other nitroaromatic compounds are 50 μM; TNT: 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene; DNBA: 3,5-Dinitrobenzoic acid; DNT: 2,4-dinitrotoluene; NA: 2-nitroaniline; NB: nitrobenzene. DMA: N,N-dimethylaniline; 0.04 M Phosphate buffer: pH 6.6; λ ex: 370 nm; λ em: 455 nm; Temperature (oC): 25; Reaction time (min): 2; All the error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.

Qualitative visual analysis of PA via FRET strategy using GCPF solid on quartz glasses (A) and using cotton swabs (B) under the irradiation of visible light (up) and 365 nm ultraviolet light (below). GCPF solid on quartz glasses, c(PA, μM): 0, 50, 100 and 200; Cotton swabs, c(PA, μM): 0, 50, 200 and 500; 0.04 M Phosphate buffer: pH 6.6; Temperature (oC): 25; Reaction time (min): 2.

Fluorescent spectra (A) and linear calibration curve (B) of PA detection using GCPF solid on quartz glasses measured via solid fluorescence scanning mode. c(PA, μM): 0, 5, 30 and 50; 0.04 M phosphate buffer: pH 6.6; λ ex: 370 nm; λ em: 395 nm; Temperature (oC): 25; Reaction time (min): 2; All the error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.
Fluorescent lifetime results by monitoring emission of GCPF at 445 nm c(PA, μM): 50; 0.04 M Phosphate buffer: pH 6.6; Temperature (oC): 25.

Schematic illustration of synthesis of fluorescent glutaraldehyde (GA) non-conjugated chitosan polymer fluorophores (GCPF) and its application in analysis of picric acid (PA) via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) strategy.
Comparison of the present work with other methods for PA detection.
Recovery results of FRET detection for PA in lake water samples using fluorescent GCPF.

head.ce:title: Glutaraldehyde non-conjugated chitosan polymer fluorophores for selective determination of picric acid via fluorescence resonance energy transfer strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Inorganic Functional Materials, College of Chemistry, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, 401331, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Inorganic Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Huize Cigarette Factory, HongyunHonghe Tabacco (Group) Co., Ltd., Huize, 654200, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-dispersed glutaraldehyde (GA) non-conjugated chitosan polymer fluorophores (GCPF) with quantum yield of 16% is synthesized by stirring chitosan and GA for 6 h at room temperature in the present work. It is a facile and mild method and fluorescent GCPF can be stabled for two months. Owing to the spectral overlap of fluorescent spectrum of GCPF and absorption spectrum of picric acid (PA), a novel sensitive fluorescent method using fluorescent GCPF for PA detection from 10 nM to 50 μM via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) strategy is established. The distance between donor of GCPF and acceptor of PA (R0 value) is calculated to be 3.5 nm. FRET method using fluorescent GCPF possesses high sensitivity (LOD of 2.8 nM), and selectivity and fast response within 2 min. Moreover, fluorescent GCPF is also utilized in visual analysis of PA using cotton swabs. Fluorescence quenching effect can be observed by eyes irradiated with 365 nm ultraviolet light at cotton swabs and using GCPF solid on quartz glasses, which paves an effect and wide way for the application of fluorescent GCPF in our daily life.

Water-dispersed glutaraldehyde (GA) non-conjugated chitosan polymer fluorophores (GCPF) with quantum yield of 16% is synthesized via a facile and mild method by stirring chitosan and GA for 6 h at room temperature. Owing to the spectral overlap of fluorescent spectrum of GCPF and absorption spectrum of picric acid (PA), a novel sensitive fluorescent method using the as-prepared GCPF for PA detection from 10 nM to 50 μM via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) strategy is established.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Polymers with fluorescence emission are generally constructed by their own conjugated main chain or π-aromatic building blocks. However, polymers usually lack water solubility. Due to their superior fluorescent properties and rich functionalities, non-conjugated polymers have recently emerged as a new class of fluorophores with wide range applications such as metal ion detection [1–3], protein sensing [4] or cellular imaging [5,6]. Fluorescence-emitting moieties of non-conjugated polymers usually contains some heteroatom double bonds (C=O, C=N, C=S, etc.) [2,4,7,8]. Although these double bonds cannot emit fluorescence under normal conditions, it is argued that macromolecules may form nanostructures under suitable conditions and bring strong fluorescent emission. Schiff base reaction derived from amino and carbonyl compounds is an effective method to obtain non-conjugated polymers fluorophores. Chitosan crosslinked with different aldehyde/carbonyl containing crosslinkers such as formaldehyde and glutaraldehyde (GA) have been utilized to synthesize this type of Schiff base non-conjugated polymers and applied in fluorescent detection of Cr (VI) [1]. Owing to the advantages of chitosan-derived non-conjugated polymers such as good biocompatiblity, low cost, ease of synthesis, nontoxicity, high photostability and superior fluorescence performance, great efforts are needed to be given to investigate the applications of chitosan-derived non-conjugated polymers.

Picric acid (PA) as an explosive with high explosive velocity and brisance is mainly used in munitions [9–12]. PA poses a potential safety threat. PA can cause strong irritation to the skin/eyes and damage organs and respiratory system. High sensitive and selective sensing of superior explosive TNP in water medium is an emerging field. There are many reported techniques on PA detection including mass spectrometry (MS) [13], surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [14], electrochemistry [15], luminescent [16,17]/photoluminescent [18,19] and fluorescent methods [20–24]. To the best of knowledge, fluorescent methods exhibit advantages such as rapid response times, simple operation, cost-effective instrumentation, high sensitivity and specificity. Therefore development of fluorescent assays for PA and improvement in specificity and selectivity is widely pursued.

Herein, a facile and mild method is designed to synthesize water-dispersed glutaraldehyde (GA) non-conjugated chitosan polymer fluorophores (GCPF) by stirring chitosan and GA for 6 h at room temperature. The as-prepared GCPF solution displays fluorescence quantum yield of 16%. Owing to the spectral overlap of fluorescent spectrum of GCPF and absorption spectrum of PA, a novel sensitive fluorescent method using fluorescent GCPF for PA detection via fluorescence resonance energy transfer strategy is established in the present work (Scheme 1 ).

Chitosan (deacetylation degree≥ 95%, crystallinity index ˃ 0.57, 100–200 mPa·s) and glutaraldehyde (GA) aqueous solution (50%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glacial acetic acid was obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Picric acid (PA), 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT); 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), 3,5-dinitrobenzoic acid (DNBA), 2-nitroaniline (NA), nitrobenzene (NB) and N,N-dimethylaniline (DMA) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, Na2SO4, NaNO3, AlCl3, CaCl2, MgCl2, ZnCl2, CuCl2, BaCl2, and CdCl2 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). All these chemicals were analytical-reagent grade and used without further purification. Phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.6, 0.2 M) was used during the experiments. Doubly distilled water was used throughout all experiments.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were taken by a SU8020 scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). 5 μL of fluorescent GCPF solution was dropped on silicon chip, dried at room temperature and undergone a spray process before SEM measurement. Fluorescent spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer Instruments, U.K.). Fluorescent photographs under 365 nm UV light were operated with ZF-20D ultraviolet analyzing equipment from Gongyi City Yuhua Instrument Co., Ltd. (Gongyi, China). Fluorescent lifetime measurements were recorded on a stable/transient fluorescence spectrometer FLS1000 (Edinburgh, England). S-25 digital pH meter (Shanghai Jingke Industry Company, Shanghai, China) was used to detect the acidity of the solution.

Typically, 1.00 g of chitosan was firstly mixed with 100.0 mL of deionized water under vigorous stirring at room temperature, followed by dropwise adding 1 mL of 1.74 M glacial acetic acid (HAc) under stirring conditions for 10 min. 0.10 mL of 4.73 M GA was then quickly injected into the above solution under constant stirring for 6 h to obtain fluorescent GCPF yellow transparent solutions.

In a 1.5 mL plastic vial, 200 μL of 0.2 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.6), different concentrations of PA and 50 μL of the obtained fluorescent GCPF were added. Then appropriate water was added to keep the final volume of the whole solution at 1 mL and thoroughly vortex mixed. Fluorescent spectra were recorded after 2 min at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of PA in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: Real lake water sample was first treated by ultra-filtration with 10 kD ultra-filtration membrane. Then 100 μL treated lake water was added into 200 μL of 0.2 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.6). Other procedures were undergone similar with the above PA detection procedure. Since no obvious PA was detected in real lake water, three different concentrations of PA (0.5 μM, 5 μM and 50 μM) were used to obtain the recovery via recovery methods of standard additions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of fluorescent GCPF
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain stable fluorescent GCPF with high fluorescent efficiency, the synthesized experimental conditions are optimized. Solvent of acetic acid (HAc) is of great importance to the solubility of chitosan, therefore effect of the amount of HAc on the synthesis of fluorescent GCPF is firstly investigated in Fig. S1. When the amount of HAc is low, the obtained fluorescent GCPF has weak emission, which may be ascribed to the low content of effective chitosan dissolved in the mixture. Finally 1 mL of 1.74 M HAc is chosen as the proper experimental condition owing to the highest fluorescent intensity of fluorescent GCPF. Next, effect of the amount of GA on the synthesis of fluorescent GCPF is optimized in Fig. S2. Higher content of GA lead to the obtained fluorescent GCPF with weak emission. Therefore 100 μL of 4.73 M GA is chosen as the optimal condition. Under the optimized synthesized conditions, the as-prepared fluorescent GCPF yellow transparent solutions show blue fluorescent emissions with maximum fluorescent emission at 445 nm and maximum fluorescent excitation at 370 nm (Fig. 1 ). Fluorescent GCPF yellow transparent solutions exhibit a quantum yield of approximately 16% by comparing quantum yield of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 [25]. The as-prepared GCPF yellow transparent solutions can be stabled for more than two months when stored in 4 °C refrigerator by detecting fluorescent intensity.

SEM characterizations were employed to monitor the morphology of the fluorescent GCPF. As shown in Fig. 1, fluorescent GCPF seems nanowires with vertical diameter of from 80 to 100 nm. Large numbers of nanowires gather together to form nanoflowers. GCPF solution has no obvious absorption peaks from 250 to 800 nm (Fig. 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of FRET detection of PA using fluorescent GCPF
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows PA has absorption from 300 nm to 450 nm and with the maximal absorption wavelength at 355 nm, which overlaps with fluorescent spectrum of GCPF Therefore FRET from fluorescent GCPF to PA occurs and the fluorescence of GCPF can be quenched after the addition of PA. To achieve sensitive and selective FRET detection of PA, effect of temperatures on FRET detection for PA is evaluated. As shown in Fig. S3, four different temperatures are used in FRET detection for TNP. After the addition of PA, nearly 70% fluorescence of GCPF is quenched indicating the feasibility of FRET principle. After analysis of fluorescent intensity, it is found that the quenching efficiency changes slightly with the increasing temperature from room temperature (25 °C) to 70 °C. To achieve facile and stable FRET detection for PA, the optimal temperature is kept at room temperature (25 °C). Effect of pH on FRET detection for PA using fluorescent GCPF is also investigated from pH 4.4 to pH 9.3 (Fig. S4). After the addition of PA to fluorescent GCPF, quenching efficiency changes slightly. However, gelatinous precipitates are formed when fluorescent GCPF under neutral or alkaline conditions from pH 7.4 to 9.3. It is speculated to poor solubility of chitosan under neutral or alkaline conditions. Chitosan contains a large amount of free amino groups which are positively charged under neutral or alkaline conditions and are therefore insoluble in water. Amino groups are protonated under acidic conditions which makes chitosan soluble in water. Therefore pH 6.6 is chosen as the optimized pH condition for following PA detection experiments owing to its nearly neutral conditions.

Fig. 3 shows kinetic behaviors of FRET detection for PA using fluorescent GCPF. It is found that fluorescent intensity of the synthesized GCPF is quenched immediately after the addition of PA. It indicates that FRET detection for PA is a fast response process nearly within 1 min. In the following times from 2 min to 30 min, fluorescent intensities of the synthesized GCPF both in the absence and presence of PA remain stable. Therefore 2 min is chosen for the following fluorescent quantitative determination of PA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of FRET detection of PA using fluorescent GCPF
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 illustrates fluorescent response of the constructed FRET platform for PA detection using fluorescent GCPF. Fluorescent intensities are decreased gradually with an increase in the concentrations of PA from 10 nM to 50 μM (Fig. 4A). There is a linear response between the concentration of PA (μM) from 10 nM to 50 μM and fluorescence quenching efficiency ((I-I 0)/I 0). The regression equation is (I-I 0)/I 0 = 0.1127 + 0.0119 c PA (μM) with correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9951 and limit of detection (LOD) of 2.8 nM. This LOD of 2.8 nM is lower than that of most other related reports about PA detection (Table 1 ) and lower than the limit of concentration of TNP in centralized sources of drinking water (GB-3838-2002, 0.005 mg/L or 21.8 nM). All of these results show that FRET detection for PA fluorescent GCPF exhibits high sensitivity.

To evaluate the selectivity of FRET method for PA detection, fluorescent spectra of GCPF in the presence of various interferences including metal ions and other nitroaromatic compounds ( Fig. 5 ). After the addition of PA to fluorescent GCPF, fluorescence of the mixtures are quenched obviously with the quenching efficiency ((I-I 0)/I 0) of nearly 70%. As reported in Ref. [1], it is true that Cr6+ has fluorescent quenching effect on GCPF. However, no obvious quenching effect is observed in the presence of other nitroaromatic compounds or other metal ions such as Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+ and other nitroaromatic compounds such as TNT, DNT, DNBA, NA, NB and DMA. These results indicate that FRET detection for PA using synthesized fluorescent GCPF exhibits high selectivity towards most of metal ions or other nitroaromatic compounds. The high selectivity is ascribed to spectral overlap of fluorescent spectrum of GCPF and absorption spectrum of PA and FRET motif from fluorescent GCPF to PA. While interferences including most of metal ions and other nitroaromatic compounds have no obvious absorbance from 350 nm to 450 nm and therefore no FRET can occur from fluorescent GCPF to these interferences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of FRET PA detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the reliability and potential application of FRET detection for PA using synthesized fluorescent GCPF, recovery experiments are performed by standard addition methods in real lake water. The recoveries of real lake water samples spiked with 0.5, 5 and 50 μM PA are obtained in Table 2 . The average recoveries of PA in the real lake water samples range from 99.3% to 101.4% with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) lower than 3%. All of them indicate that FRET detection for PA using synthesized fluorescent GCPF possesses good promise in analysis of practical samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FRET detection of PA using cotton swabs and GCPF solid on quartz glasses
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows simple method for fluorescent visual qualitative detection of PA using cotton swabs and GCPF solid on quartz glasses measured. GCPF solid on quartz glasses displays blue fluorescence irradiated with 365 nm ultraviolet light. In the presence of PA from 50 μM to 200 μM, blue fluorescence of GCPF solid is gradually quenched (Fig. 6A). The fluorescent quenching qualitative detection of PA is observed after cotton swabs irradiated with 365 nm ultraviolet light. In the presence of PA from 50 μM to 500 μM, blue fluorescence is gradually quenched (Fig. 6B). GCPF solid is also applied in analysis of PA. Fig. 7 shows fluorescent gradually decreasing of GCPF solid on quartz glasses measured via solid fluorescence scanning mode in the presence of PA from 5 to 50 μM. The sensitivity of GCPF solid method for PA detection is lower than GCPF solution detection (Table 1) but higher than qualitative detection of PA using cotton swabs. Both cotton swabs and GCPF solid on quartz glasses are readily available in daily life, which paves an effect and wide way for the application of fluorescent GCPF in our daily life. Therefore it is a novel fast, simple and visual detection of PA via FRET strategy using fluorescent GCPF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of PA detection using fluorescent GCPF
ce:section.ce:para:
According to reported FRET references [26–30], fluorescent spectrum of GCPF overlaps with absorption spectrum of PA which is in accordance with the principle of FRET. Besides, the overlap integral (J) and Förster distance (R0) is also calculated by utilizing the extinction coefficient of PA according to the literature [31,32]. The overlap integral J is calculated as described by equation (1), where F D(λ) and ε A(λ) are the normalized emission of the donor (GCPF) and absorption coefficient of the acceptor (PA), respectively. With this data and the molar extinction coefficient (ε) of TNP 1.25 × 104 M−1⋅ cm−1, J is calculated to be 3.25 × 10−14 M−1 cm−1 cm4 in water.
(1)
J ( λ ) =
∫
0
∞
F
D
( λ )
ε
A
( λ )
λ
4
d λ
(2)
R
0
6
= 8.79 ×
10
− 5
k
2
Φ D J ( λ )
n
4

In equation (2), n is the refractive index of water (1.34) and κ is a factor describing the relative orientation in space of the transition dipoles of the donor and acceptor, assumed to be κ 2 = 2/3 for dynamic random averaging of the donor and acceptor.

In conclusion, fluorescent water-dispersed GCPF with quantum yield of 16% is synthesized by stirring chitosan and GA for 6 h at room temperature in the present work. It is a facile and mild method. Fluorescent GCPF can be stabled for two months. It has been applied in FRET detection of PA with high sensitivity (LOD of 2.8 nM), and selectivity and fast response within 2 min. Moreover, fluorescent GCPF is also utilized in visual analysis of PA using cotton swabs. Both fluorescent GCPF solution and solid show fluorescent quenching effect in FRET detection of PA, which paves an effect and wide way for the application of fluorescent GCPF in our daily life.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21505011), Chongqing Research Program of Basic Research and Frontier Technology (No. cstc2018jcyjAX0742) and Chongqing Normal University Youth Talent Development Program (02030307-00042).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

Supplementary data including the optimized synthesis of fluorescent GCPF, effect of temperature and pH on PA detection are free available on the website.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2019.01.043.
